description: The scent of roasting coffee beans and frankincense fills the morning air in Addis Ababa, as the city wakes to the sound of church bells and the call to prayer. The streets pulse with life - diplomats in suits share sidewalks with women in traditional habesha kemis, while the aroma of injera and wat drifts from hole-in-the-wall restaurants. The city's skyline tells its story - from the ancient rock-hewn churches to the modern African Union headquarters, from the National Museum where Lucy's bones rest to the jazz clubs where Ethio-jazz legends play. In the Merkato, Africa's largest open-air market, the sound of Amharic haggling mixes with the scent of spices and fresh leather, while in the diplomatic quarter, the flags of nations flutter in the highland breeze. Addis Ababa's power lies in this convergence of past and present, where ancient traditions meet pan-African aspirations, and where every cup of coffee tells a story of Ethiopia's rich heritage.
